coredata.dc:description: Research has shown that carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are active as novel carbon nanomaterials in photodynamic therapy due to their excellent photophysical properties. However, previously expensive precursors and time-consuming production processes, as well as complex doping/functionalization forms have limited their economic design and use. In this study, we prepared ST-JHCQDs by a simple and green method using natural plant turmeric as the carbon source, and characterized ST-JHCQDs by physical and optical means. In vitro antibacterial results showed that the antibacterial effects of ST-JHCQDs against E. coli and S. aureus under the irradiation of blue light depended on carbonization degrees, concentration and light duration. Biomolecule leakage and confocal laser scanning microscope analysis showed that ST-JHCQDs were effective in producing reactive oxygen species (ROS) under blue light irradiation, which resulted in disturbance of cell membrane integrity and leakage of intracellular macromolecules in both bacteria. Meanwhile, scanning electron microscopy images showed cell membrane wrinkling and fragmentation, which was consistent with ROS damage, demonstrating the effectiveness of ST-JHCQDs as photosensitizers in vitro photodynamic antimicrobial activity. These experiments show that CQDs prepared from turmeric was a new natural photosensitizer material with great antibacterial potential.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of carbon quantum dots from plant turmeric holds promise as novel photosensitizer for in vitro photodynamic antimicrobial activity

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Research has shown that carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are active as novel carbon nanomaterials in photodynamic therapy due to their excellent photophysical properties. However, previously expensive precursors and time-consuming production processes, as well as complex doping/functionalization forms have limited their economic design and use. In this study, we prepared ST-JHCQDs by a simple and green method using natural plant turmeric as the carbon source, and characterized ST-JHCQDs by physical and optical means. In vitro antibacterial results showed that the antibacterial effects of ST-JHCQDs against E. coli and S. aureus under the irradiation of blue light depended on carbonization degrees, concentration and light duration. Biomolecule leakage and confocal laser scanning microscope analysis showed that ST-JHCQDs were effective in producing reactive oxygen species (ROS) under blue light irradiation, which resulted in disturbance of cell membrane integrity and leakage of intracellular macromolecules in both bacteria. Meanwhile, scanning electron microscopy images showed cell membrane wrinkling and fragmentation, which was consistent with ROS damage, demonstrating the effectiveness of ST-JHCQDs as photosensitizers in vitro photodynamic antimicrobial activity. These experiments show that CQDs prepared from turmeric was a new natural photosensitizer material with great antibacterial potential.

ce:section.ce:para: Traditional antimicrobial drugs for microorganisms mainly include antibiotics, quinolones, sulfonamides and other chemically synthesized drugs. While these drugs have revolutionized medicine, the misuse of antibiotics in recent years has contributed to microbial resistance to antibiotics and the development of resistance that has posed and continues to pose a serious challenge to the human living environment, food safety, and health care systems [1,2]. Thus, forcing the development of effective antimicrobial materials as alternatives to these drugs to face the world health safety problems posed by microorganisms is intentional research.

With the advancement of science and technology in modern society, nanoscience has developed into a new scientific field of great interest. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are the newest member of carbon-based nanomaterials and are considered to be one of the rising stars due to their unique physical and optical properties [3–5]. CQDs are typically smaller than 10 nm in size, making it one of the smallest nanomaterials. In addition, compared to the inherently toxic metal-based semiconductor quantum dots (Cd, Pb, In), most CQDs exhibit good biocompatibility, and low toxicity, paving the way for the subsequent use of CQDs in a variety of therapeutic applications such as antibacterial [6,7], anticancer [8], drug delivery [9], bioimaging [10,11], antiviral [12,13] and wound healing [14]. So far, various methods have been used to prepare CQDs, including top-down and bottom-up approaches. The former involves the decomposition of larger precursors, while the latter includes the carbonization of organic precursors [15,16]. The bottom-up approach allows the physicochemical properties of CQDs, such as hydrophobicity, particle size, optical properties and surface functional groups, to be tuned by judicious selection of molecular precursors and synthesis steps Facile and Purification-Free Synthesis of Nitrogenated Amphiphilic Graphitic Carbon Dots [17,18]. Meanwhile, CQDs exhibit satisfactory photodynamic antimicrobial effects under visible/natural light activation due to their broad spectral absorption in the ultraviolet region to near-infrared region.

Photodynamic therapy (PDT) is a series of reactions in which photosensitizers (PSs) were activated by light and combined with oxygen molecules to produce cytotoxic ROS (superoxide anion (O2-), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), hydroxyl radicals (-OH) and singlet oxygen (1O2) [19,20]. It has been shown that ROS cause irreversible bacterial death by inducing oxidative stress that damages cellular DNA/RNA or directly causes damage to the outer membrane of bacteria [21–23]. Secondly, PDT is considered as a revolutionary minimally invasive treatment with significant advantages such as low trauma, low toxicity, high accuracy, no drug resistance and fast healing of healthy tissues [24–27].

Many different pure compounds have been used alone or in combination to synthesize CQDs with PDT, and researchers have also added different atomic compounds (N, S and P) have also been added in the preparation process to prepare CQDs with different properties and functions. The doping of heteroatoms alters the electronic environment inside the CQDs and increases the surface defects of the CQDs to facilitate the effective separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, thus enhancing the photodynamic therapeutic effect of CQDs [28–32]. The nitrogen-doped and amino-modified CQDs synthesized by Huang and Yang et al. can be used as two-photon PSs and have a higher 1O2 yield than the unmodified CQDs [33]. CQDs have been found to be easily modified with positively charge or quaternary ammonium group, which endowed them ability to effectively interact with the negatively charged structure of bacteria and therefore improve the antibacterial performance of CQDs [7,34–37]. Secondly, the surface passivation of CQDs, fluorescence quantum yield, type of oxidation reductant and change of emission excitation state are also important factors affecting CQDs-mediated PDT. When organic precursors form core carbon nanoparticles, their carbonization is incomplete and the remaining organic species serve the surface passivation function required for CQDs. For example, under mild hydrothermal conditions, CQDs prepared with the antibiotic ciprofloxacin or penicillin as precursors all retain some antibiotic structure [38,39]. In addition, CQDs can also be used as a carrier of antibiotics or photosensitizers to prepare a series of functionalized carbon points. Jijie et al. prepared CDs-AMP by linking amino groups on the surface of CQDs with Ampicillin (AMP) [40]. Sidhu et al. took Penicillin G as a carbon source and Penicillin bonded with amino carbon points through amide bonds to prepare PCDs and CDs-penicillin functional carbon points for comparison [39]. These antimicrobial results of CQDs under visible light were attributed to antibiotic maintenance and/or ROS production. Chen et al. created a fluorescence energy resonance transfer system of CDs-Ce6 by combining Chlorin e6 (Ce6), a photosensitizer, with corresponding spectral matching CQDs through covalent bonds, enhancing the photodynamic effect of Ce6 [41]. Our group reported CQDs nano-composites that prepared by combining CQDs with curcumin showed effective photodynamic antibacterial action against both Gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria [42].

However, the doping of pure compounds and multiple atoms increases the production cost of CQDs. In addition, the surface modification and the addition of antibiotics and photosensitizers further aggravate the complexity of the preparation of surface functionalized CQDs and the difficulty of purification. As a result, it is difficult to mass produce these CQDs and use them in practical treatment. A more cost-effective alternative is to put a price on synthetic natural sources. It is worth paying attention to the synthesis of CQDs from natural, non-toxic, inexhaustible and inexpensive precursors and their application prospect in various fields [9,43]. Due to the economy of natural resources and mild conditions of use, the synthesis of these CQDs is considered green in many cases. Selection of different green precursors or different synthesis routes will affect the performance of CQDs, thus affecting the applicability of its application [44–47].

Curcumin is a kind of bioactive natural compound isolated from curcuma. Turmeric contains 3–5% curcumin derivatives-curcumin (70%), demethoxylcurcumin (20%) and dimethoxylcurcumin (10%), of which curcumin is the most important component, giving it its yellow color. Curcumin is also currently used in PDT as an effective photosensitizer against a variety of bacteria [48,49]. Based on the extraction of curcumin rich in photosensitive chemical components, ST-JHCQDs was obtained by solvent method (Scheme 1 ). In vitro photodynamic antibacterial performance of ST-JHCQDs as photosensitizers against Gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria was evaluated by changing carbonization temperature. Different techniques were used to characterize the surface morphology, structural characteristics, optical properties and photodynamic antibacterial mechanism of ST-JHCQDs. More importantly, compared with CQDs prepared by other pure compounds and complex surface functionalization and loading forms, the precursor of ST-JHCQDs has abundant sources, simple preparation route and environmental protection, which is more suitable for large-scale production, and is expected to be used as a new photosensitizer to combat the world health security problems caused by bacteria.

2′, 7-di-chlorofluorescin diacetate (DCFH-DA) were procured from Aladdin Industrial Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). 4′, 6-Diamidino-2-phenylindole Dihydrochloride (DAPI) and Propidium iodide (PI) was procured from Solarbio Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Nutrient Agar and Nutrient Broth were purchased from Beijing Land Bridge Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Escherichia coli and Staphylococcus aureus were purchased from Shanghai Luwei Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China).

The turmeric was purchased from a local pharmacy. Turmeric was put into a pulverize and ground to a powder, then transferred to an oven at 60 °C to dry. The dried turmeric powder (2.0 g) was mixed with ethanol solution (30 mL) and stirred continuously for 30 min under ultrasound. Finally, the solution was centrifuged at 8000 r/min for 10 min, and the supernatant was filtered by 0.22 μm microporous membrane to further remove the large solids.

Turmeric extract (20 mL) was transferred to into a 25 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 10 h in an oven. After the reaction liquid was cooled to room temperature, the clarified solution was collected by centrifugation and 0.22 μm microporous membrane successively, and then ethanol was concentrated by rotary evaporation, and ethyl acetate and petroleum ether were added at the same time to precipitate. The precipitation was dried in an oven at 60 °C to obtain carbon dots powder (ST-JHCQDs). Under the same carbonization time of 10 h, the carbonization temperature was raised to 200 °C, 220 °C and 240 °C, respectively, and carbon dots with different degree of carbonization were prepared.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images of ST-JHCQDs at 200 kV were recorded by Talos F200X G2 (Hitachi Tokyo, Japan) system, and the size of ST-JHCQDs particle size was analyzed by nano measurement software 1.2. Prior to TEM, ST-JHCQDs was usually dispersed in anhydrous ethanol, and the diluted carbon dots solution was gradually dripped onto a 300-mesh carbon-coated copper grid to volatilize the excess ethanol at room temperature. X-ray electron diffractometer (XRD-X'Pert PRO MPD; Rigaku, Japan) was used to determine the crystal shape of ST-JHCQDs, and the parameters were set as: scanning voltage 40 kV, current 40 mA, scanning speed 10°. The specific composition of functional groups on the surface of ST-JHCQDs and the proportion of each element were obtained by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS-Thermo fisher Scientific K-Alpha). The potential of the ST-JHCQDs was recorded by a nano-particle potentiometer (ZSE; Malvern Instruments Co. Ltd, Britain). The ultraviolet absorption and fluorescence properties of ST-JHCQDs were analyzed by ultraviolet spectrophotometer (UV–5200PC; Shanghai metash Instruments Co. Ltd, China) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (RF-6000; Shimadzu, Japan) respectively. The surface functional groups and structures of ST-JHCQDs were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR-iS10; Thermo Nicolet Ltd, USA) using potassium bromide tablet method.

First, refrigerated bacteria were inoculated in 4 mL of nutrient broth culture medium and incubated overnight in a constant temperature incubator at 37 °C and 200 r/min. Secondly, the activated suspension was transferred to the centrifuge tube, centrifuged at 5500 r/min for 5 min, removed the culture medium, and cleaned twice by adding 0.9% NaCl. Finally, the bacteria were suspended again with 0.9% NaCl, and the absorbance was adjusted to OD600 = 0.5 by UV-spectrophotometer for use. Gram-positive bacteria and gram-negative bacteria were Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Escherichia coli (E. coli), respectively. 405 nm (20 mW/cm2) light source was selected as photodynamic antibacterial light source. Glassware and Agar plate used in the experiment were sterilized for 20 min at 120 °C in autoclave.

ST-JHCQDs was first dissolved in anhydrous ethanol (5 mg/mL) and then diluted to different concentrations with 0.9% NaCl. Then, ST-JHCQDs (90 μL) solution at various concentrations was mixed with bacterial suspensions (10 μL) to reach the final ST-JHCQDs concentrations of 0.5, 5 and 50 μM, respectively (ethanol content in the mixed solution was less than 5%). The mixed bacterial suspensions were placed in a 37 °C incubator for 30 min, and then irradiated with blue light (405 nm, 20 mW/cm2) for 30 min, while mixed bacterial suspensions without light served as a dark control. Next, 30 μL of mixed bacterial suspensions was plated on LB agar plates, and the number of bacterial colonies was counted and recorded after incubation for 24 h. All experiments were performed three times.

Similarly, ST-JHCQDs was diluted to the specified concentration with 0.9% NaCl, and ST-JHCQDs (90 μL) solution was mixed with bacterial suspensions (90 μL) (ethanol content in the mixed solution was less than 5%), respectively. The final concentration of ST-JHCQDs in E. coli was 50 μg/mL, while that in S. aureus was 5 μg/mL. The mixed bacterial suspensions were placed in a 37 °C incubator for 30 min, and then the mixed bacterial suspension was successively irradiated with blue light (405 nm, 20 mW/cm2) for 0, 5, 10, 20 and 40 min. 0.9% NaCl incubated bacteria were exposed to blue light for 40 min as a blank control (without ST-JHCQDs). Next, 30 μL of mixed bacterial suspensions was plated on LB agar plates, and the number of bacterial colonies was counted and recorded after incubation for 24 h. All experiments were performed three times.

The leakage of biomolecules assay was performed based on the reported method [50]. In brief, the bacterial suspensions (OD600 = 0.7) were mixed with JHCQDs and exposed under blue light irradiation for 40 min, and then incubated on a shaker at 37 °C for 4 h (E. coli: 50 μg/mL, S. aureus: 5 μg/mL). The supernatant of the mixture was collected by centrifugation and 0.2 μm microporous membrane, and the absorbance at OD260 and OD280 was measured.

DCHF-DA was used to detect the production of reactive oxygen species by ST-JHCQDs combined with blue light in bacterial cells. Bacteria were treated and centrifuged as mentioned in Section 2.5.1. First, the bacterial suspensions (OD600 = 0.5) were mixed with ST-JHCQDs (50 μg/mL) and exposed under blue light irradiation for 40 min. Secondly, after illumination, the bacteria were washed with PBS and added of DCFH-DA (20 μM), and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min in darkness. The bacteria were washed with PBS again and collect, then 200 μL of 0.9% NaCl was added to suspend bacteria. Finally, 10 μL of bacterial suspension was dropped into a confocal dish to be imaged with a TCS PS5Ⅱ confocal laser scanning microscope (Leica).

The integrity of bacterial membrane was analyzed by DAPI and PI fluorescence probes. Bacterial suspension (1 mL, OD600 = 0.5) was centrifuged to obtain the bacteria, and 200 μL of ST-JHCQDs was added to E. coli and S. aureus, respectively. The bacteria treated with ST-JHCQDs combined with blue light irradiation for 40 min were washed twice by PBS, after 200 μL of DAPI/PI solution was added (concentrations of DAPI and PI were 10 μM and 20 μM), the bacteria was incubated in darkness at 37 °C for 15 min, and then PBS was added to clean and disperse the bacteria. Finally, 10 μL of bacterial suspension was dropped into a confocal dish to be imaged with a TCS PS5Ⅱ confocal laser scanning microscope (Leica).

The morphological changes of bacteria under ST-JHCQDs combined with blue light were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The bacteria incubated at ST-JHCQDs were washed twice by 0.9% NaCl after blue light irradiation and collected. Then GLUTA solution of 40 times the volume of bacteria was added and fixed overnight in a refrigerator at 4 °C to maintain the surface morphology of bacteria. The fixed bacterial solution was washed with 0.01 mol/L PBS buffer, and then dehydrated with 30%, 50%, 70%, 80% and 90% ethanol, respectively, and 100% ethanol twice. After gradient dehydration, the bacteria were dispersed in tert-butanol solution, and 10 μL of mixed bacterial suspension drops were placed on the glass slide. Finally, the bacteria were freeze-dried and sprayed with platinum, and SEM images were generated by the HITACHI SU3500 scanning electron microscope.

Bacterial labeling imaging study were conducted based on reported methods [51]. Bacteria were treated and centrifuged as mentioned in Section 2.5.1, and 2 mL of bacteria was taken from Eppendorf tube, centrifuged at 5000 r/min for 5 min and then cleaned with PBS twice. Finally, the obtained bacteria were resuspended in 100 μL of PBS with 100 μL of ST-JHST-JHCQDs (50 μg/mL) and incubated for 4 h. An amount of 10 μL of the samples was deposited on confocal dish and allowed to air-dry for Confocal microscopy imaging.

Human normal hepatocytes (L-02) in the logarithmic growth phase were employed to detect the in vitro cytotoxicity of ST-JHST-JHCQDs using a CCK-8 kit. Cell suspension (200 μL) containing 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS) and 1640 nutrient solution was added to each well of 96-well plates and incubated for 24 h in an incubator at 37 °C with 5% CO2. Then, the cells were treated with ST-JHCQDs for 48 h at 37 °C with 5% CO2. CCK-8 (5%, 200 μL) was added to each well and the cell solution was incubated for 3 h. At the end of incubation, the absorbance at a wavelength of 450 nm was measured using a microplate reader (Epoch2, BioTek).

Blood from the mouse eyeballs was removed and placed in a centrifuge tube containing sodium citrate. The sticky blood was removed by gently stirring the tube with a cotton swab. The treated blood was centrifuged three times, and 2% red blood cell suspension (RBC) was prepared by 0.9% NaCl solution. 500 μL of 2% RBCs was mixed with 500 μL of ST-JHST-JHCQDs solution of different concentrations. Then, the red blood cells were placed in a water bath at 37 °C for incubation for 1 h, centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min, and 200 μL of supernatant was taken into a 96-well plate, and its absorbance value was measured at 545 nm wavelength. (Infinite M200 Pro; Tecan, Switzerland). The red blood cells treated with 0.9% NaCl solutions and ultrapure water set as negative control and positive control, respectively.
Calculation of hemolysis rate: hemolysis% = (sample absorbance-negative control absorbance)/(positive control absorbance-negative control absorbance) × 100%

The synthesis process of ST-JHCQDs mainly includes the extraction of turmeric and solvothermal treatment. Compared with other methods of carbon point preparation, this experiment prepared ST-JHCQDs simply and green by natural carbon source (turmeric) and environmentally friendly solvent (ethanol). At the same time, by changing the carbonization temperature (such as 180 °C, 200 °C, 220 °C and 240 °C) under the same carbonization time, ST-JHST-JHCQDs with different degrees of carbonization were obtained, which were denoted as ST-JHCQDs-180, ST-JHCQDs-200, ST-JHCQDs-220 and ST-JHCQDs-240, respectively.

TEM and nano measurement software indicated that ST-JHCQDs-180 was a round particle with uniform particle size distribution, with a particle size range of 6–16 nm and an average particle size of 10.7 nm (Fig. 1 a–b). The Zeta potential of ST-JHCQDs-180 recorded by Malvin particle Size measuring instrument in 0.9% NaCl was −17.5 mV (Fig. 1b inset). In the past report, Lin et al. found that the size of CQDs increased from 4.2 nm to 5.2 nm when the carbonization temperature was increased from 120 °C to 210 °C and concluded that the high temperature led to the formation of larger polymer clusters or carbon cores without any change in their morphology [52]. Sarkar et al. also found that increasing the synthesis temperature not only increases the size of the CQDs, but also changes the Zeta potential of the CQDs, which may be related to the dispersion due to the size of the CQDs [53]. Therefore, we infer that different preparation temperatures have the same effect on the morphology, morphology and Zeta potential of ST-JHCQDs.

Amorphous carbon is characterized by a close arrangement of carbon atoms with alkyl chains. According to XRD patterns, ST-JHCQDs with different carbonization degrees all have a wide and significant peak at 19.18, indicating that ST-JHCQDs-180, ST-JHCQDs-200, ST-JHCQDs-220 and ST-JHCQDs-240 are all amorphous carbon components (Fig. 1c) [54]. The chemical compositions and bands of ST-JHCQDs are further characterized. As shown in the FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1d), the absorption peaks of these ST-JHCQDs were located near 3428 cm−1 and 2941 cm−1, which were attributed to the stretching vibration of O–H and C–H respectively. In addition, the peak at around 1604 cm−1 and 1519 cm−1 were caused by stretching vibration of CO and CC, respectively. There is an adsorption peak at 1276 cm−1 as a result of the bending vibration of C–O–C [55,56].

The elemental composition and chemical states of ST-JHCQDs at four different carbonization temperatures were characterized by XPS. As shown in Fig. 2 a, ST-JHCQDs-180, ST-JHCQDs-200, ST-JHCQDs-220 and ST-JHCQDs-240 all have two peaks near 284.5 and 532.0 eV, which corresponding to C1s and O1s, respectively [56]. Taking ST-JHCQDs-180 as an example, three peaks located at 284.50, 286.30 and 288.56 eV in the C1s spectra, which were attributed to C–C/CC, C–O and CO respectively (Fig. 2b) [12]. In the O1s spectra, two characteristic peaks at 531.96 and 533.25 were attributed to CO and C–O/O–H respectively, confirming the existence of carbon and oxygen compounds (Fig. 2f–i) [57,58]. The characteristic peaks of other ST-JHCQDs with different carbonization degrees were consistent with ST-JHCQDs-180 in C1s spectra and O1s spectra (Fig. 2b–i). Although the structures of these ST-JHCQDs are familiar, the number of chemical bonds is different. With the increase of carbonization temperature, the content of C–C/CC decreased and the content of C–O increased in C1s XPS spectra, indicating the increase of phenolic hydroxyl or ether bonds on the surface of ST-JHCQDs. However, in O1s spectrum, the key link mode of carbon and oxygen on the surface of carbon point is changed from double bond to single bond, with the emphasis on the reduction of CO content and the increase of C–O/O–H content.

The UV–visible absorption spectrum of ST-JHCQDs is shown in Fig. 3 . The absorption peak at 420 nm can be attributed to the functionalization of curcumin molecules, based on the large amount of curcumin contained in turmeric [59]. With the increase of carbonization temperature, the absorption peak of ST-JHCQDs at 420 nm gradually decreased until it disappeared. ST-JHCQDs-180 showed additional absorption peaks at 233 nm and 326 nm, which were caused by π-π∗ (CC) and n-π∗ (CO) transitions, respectively (Fig. 3a). With the increase of carbonization temperature, diffraction shoulder peaks of ST-JHCQDs-200, ST-JHCQDs-220 and JHCQDs-240 at 285 nm were gradually prominent, and absorption peaks at 320 nm showed a downward trend, which was due to the high carbonization of the precursor (turmeric extract) forming large graphite carbon nuclei at higher temperature (Fig. 3b–d). Reduction of curcumin molecules on ST-JHCQDs surface results in n-π∗ (CO) transition [56].

At the same time, we also used fluorescence emission spectrum to analyze ST-JHCQDs. When the excitation wavelength was changed from 320 nm to 480 nm, the emission wavelength of ST-JHCQDs-180 and ST-JHCQDs-200 was almost unchanged, which was consistent with the reported fluorescence emission spectrum of curcumin (Fig. 4 a–b). On the contrary, the emission wavelength of ST-JHCQDs-200 and ST-JHCQDs-240 appeared red shift (Fig. 4c–d). The photoluminescence properties of carbon dots are not only dependent on particle size, but also on the surface state [40,60]. Combined with the performance of carbon points in UV absorption spectrum, we speculated that with the increase of carbonization temperature, curcumin molecules began to over-carbonize into carbon nuclei, resulting in the transformation of fluorescent emission groups on the surface of carbon points from curcumin molecules to carbon nuclei. Thus, curcumin in turmeric can be used both as a carbon source and as a surface-functionalized molecule in ST-JHCQDs by solvothermal methods, and the molecule is affected by the charring temperature.

Fluorescence stability of ST-JHCQDs plays a key role in many applications. As shown in Fig. 5 a, with the increase of carbonization temperature during the preparation of ST-JHCQDs, the fluorescence stability of ST-JHCQDs transitions from acidic environment to alkaline environment. Stability in the broad pH range of 5–10 is a constructive property for a fluorescence probe to be helpful in biological systems. In contrast, ST-JHCQDs-180 and ST-JHCQDs-200 are more suitable for application in biological domains. The stability of ST-JHCQDs was further studied in high salt conditions, and ST-JHCQDS-220 and ST-JHCQDs-240 were more capable of resisting high ionic strengths (Fig. 5b). The above results indicated that the carbonization temperature during the preparation process had a certain influence on the fluorescence stability of ST-JHCQDs in high ionic strength and pH solution.

Curcumin is well known as a natural photosensitizer, and its photodynamic antibacterial properties have been reported in many articles. Through the analysis of optical and structural characterization of ST-JHCQDs, we speculated that curcumin in turmeric extract endowed ST-JHCQDs with photodynamic antibacterial effect. Considering that the photodynamic effect of ST-JHCQDs was related to the carbonization temperature, we selected ST-JHCQDs-180, ST-JHCQDs-200 and ST-JHCQDs-220 as experimental drugs, and the turmeric extract (without solvothermal method, ST-JH) as the control drug to evaluate the photodynamic antibacterial performance of ST-JHCQDs. Experiments were performed at a fixed blue light intensity of 20 mW/cm 2 (wavelength: 405 nm).

As shown in Fig. 6 a–b, under blue light irradiation for 40 min, ST-JHCQDs showed significant photodynamic bactericidal effects against both S. aureus and E. coli with increasing concentrations of ST-JHCQDs, which were more sensitive to S. aureus. In dark conditions, ST-JH and ST-JHCQDs-180 at 5 μg/mL inactivated 51% and 55% of S. aureus, respectively, and their inactivation rates against S. aureus increased to 99.9% under light. Due to the rich content of curcumin in turmeric, and the antibacterial activity of curcumin has been reported several times, so ST-JH and ST-JHCQDs-180 have some antibacterial effect under dark [48]. The inactivation of S. aureus by ST-JHCQDs-200 and ST-JHCQDs-220 combined with light also increased from 54% to 57%–99.9%, respectively, when the concentration of ST-JHCQDs increased to 50 μg/mL. Compared to S. aureus, ST-JHCQDs-180 combined with blue light was required at a concentration of 50 μg/mL to successfully kill 99.9% of E. coli, while ST-JHCQDs-180 only killed 26% of E. coli under the dark. In addition, 98%, 99% and 70% of E. coli were inactivated by 50 μg/mL of ST-JH, ST-JHCQDs-200 and ST-JHCQDs-220 in the light, respectively, which were higher than their inactivation rates in the dark (32%, 38% and 33%). In a representative plot of the agar plates, we can visualize that the photodynamic antibacterial properties of ST-JHCQDs against S. aureus and E. coli increased with increasing concentration of ST-JHCQDs and decreased with increasing carbonation temperature of ST-JHCQDs (Fig. 6c–d).

The photodynamic antibacterial activity of ST-JHCQDs under different blue light irradiation times was also investigated (Fig. 7 ). The inactivation of S. aureus by 5 μg/mL of ST-JH, ST-JHCQDs-180, ST-JHCQDs-200 and ST-JHCQDs-220 increased from 70%, 87%, 66% and 40%–99.9%, 99.9%, 99% and 91%, respectively, as the irradiation time was extended from 5 min to 40 min. Similarly, the inactivation of E. coli by 50 μg/mL of ST-JH, ST-JHCQDs-180, ST-JHCQDs-200 and ST-JHCQDs-220 was increased from 94%, 51%, 58% and 21%–98%, 99.9%, 99% and 70%, respectively. (Fig. 7a–b). It is interesting to note that 5 μg/mL of ST-JHCQDs-180 inactivated 99.9% of S. aureus with only 20 min of light exposure. In the fight against E. coli, only 50 μg/mL of ST-JHCQDs-180 inactivated 99.9% of E. coli when the illumination time was 40 min. In a representative graph of agar plates, the colony counts of ST, ST-JHCQDs-180 and ST-JHCQDs-200 treated S. aureus and E. coli decreased significantly with increasing light time, while the colony counts of ST-JHCQDs-220 treated bacteria only slightly decreased (Fig. 7c–d).

The antimicrobial performance of photodynamic therapy relies heavily on the amount of ROS production, and prolonged irradiation is laterally beneficial to increase the amount of ROS production or prolong the presence of ROS, so the duration of irradiation becomes one of the main factors affecting the antimicrobial activity of photodynamic [50,61]. Therefore, prolonging the irradiation time of ST-JHCQDs is beneficial to improve their antimicrobial efficacy. Combined with the previous contents, in addition to the time of illumination, we also concluded that the antibacterial properties of ST-JHCQDs combined with blue light are dependent on the carbonization temperature and concentration of ST-JHCQDs. The prepared ST-JHCQDs were more effective against Gram-positive S. aureus than Gram-negative E. coli. In previous studies, it has been reported that the photodynamic technique possesses different selectivity for Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria because of their different cell surface structures. Usually, photosensitizers are more likely to penetrate the porous cell walls of Gram-positive bacteria to exert photodynamic antibacterial effects than the outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria to act as a barrier [62,63].

An important mechanism of photodynamic technology-induced bacterial death stems from impaired cell membrane integrity, which was assessed by measuring the leakage of intracellular biomolecules (proteins and nucleic acids), which have the largest absorption peaks at 260 nm and 280 nm, respectively [50,64]. Since ST-CQDs has absorbance at 260 nm and 280 nm, we only compared the leakage of bacteria inclusions before and after illumination. Bacteria treated in dark conditions served as a control group, and the results showed that ST-JHCQDs-180, ST-JHCQDs-200 and ST-JHCQDs-220 were all able to cause damage to the cell membranes of S. aureus and E. coli under the activation of blue light, which resulted in the leakage of biomolecules (Fig. 8 a). By comparing OD260 and OD280 in each sample, the leakage of bacterial inclusions was found to be ST-JHCQDs-180 > ST-JH > ST-JHCQDs-200 > ST-JHCQDs-220 (Fig. 8b).

ROS generation contains singlet oxygen, superoxide and hydroxyl groups and is an important step in the destruction of bacterial cell membranes by PDT action [19,65,66]. The oxidative stress response within the bacteria was analyzed by DCFH-DA staining, which was converted to green fluorescent 2,7 dichlorofluorescein (DCF) in the presence of ROS. As shown in Fig. 9 , an intense green fluorescence was observed inside S. aureus, indicating that 50 μg/mL of ST-JH, ST-JHCQDs-180, ST-JHCQDs-200 and ST-JHCQDs-220 were all able to produce large amounts of ROS in the presence of light. In contrast, only a small amount of green fluorescence was observed in the control and ST-JHCQDs-treated S. aureus in the dark. A similar situation was visualized in E. coli, with the difference that only a small amount of green fluorescence was observed in E. coli treated with 50 μg/mL of ST-JHCQDs-220 in combination with light, further confirming the sensitivity of ST-JHCQDs to S. aureus compared with E. coli.

Bacteria were stained with DAPI and PI to further investigate the disruption of bacterial cell membranes by ST-JHCQDs under blue light (Fig. 10 ). Neither Control (Dark and Light) nor ST-JHCQDs (Dark) treated E. coli and S. aureus showed large amounts of red fluorescence by confocal microscopy, indicating that the cell membrane was not significantly disrupted and the bacteria remained active [67]. ST-JH, ST-JHCQDs-180 and ST-JHCQDs-200 combined with light treated E. coli and S. aureus all showed large amounts of red fluorescence and overlapped with blue fluorescence, indicating that bacterial cell membranes were damaged. Secondly, ST-JHCQDs-220 treated bacteria only showed a small amount of red fluorescence, indicating that ST-JHCQDs mediated DPT is related to the degree of carbonization. In addition, some of the red fluorescence in the dark conditions came from the fluorescence emission of ST-JH and ST-JHCQDs themselves.

In order to be able to visually analyze the effect of ST-JHCQDs on bacteria in the presence of blue light, the morphology of bacteria was observed using SEM (Fig. 11 ). The control group was the typical size and shape of the bacteria: E. coli was rod-shaped bacterium, while S. aureus have a spherical appearance, both with intact surface morphology. When the bacteria were incubated with ST-JHCQDs-180, ST-JHCQDs-200 and ST-JHCQDs-220, respectively, the cell structure of both bacteria was significantly disrupted under blue light irradiation, including surface deformation, volume loss, and/or a flattening of the cells, all of which strongly suggest cell membrane damage and leakage of contents [68,69]. Overall, SEM images and confocal images showed that ST-JHCQDs can generate ROS to inactivate bacteria under irradiation, which is consistent with other photodynamic antibacterial mechanisms reported in the literature, and well demonstrates that ST-JHCQDs can be used as PSs and applied in photodynamic antibacterial studies.

On the one hand, since CQDs have been commonly used for in vitro and in vivo bioimaging applications [51]. On the other hand, ST-JHCQDs-180 possess higher emission fluorescence intensity among ST-JHCQDs prepared at different temperatures (Fig. 4a–d). Therefore, ST-JHCQDs-180 was used as a representative for fluorescent labeling imaging of S. aureus and E. coli. As shown in Fig. 12 a–b, the Control group showed no fluorescence production, and the bacteria incubated with ST-JHCQDs-180 showed more intense green fluorescence relative to ST-JH, with E. coli being the most obvious. Meanwhile, we collected bacteria from ST-JHCQDs-180 (or ST-JH) after incubation by centrifugation and measured the fluorescence intensity of bacteria resuspended in 0.9% NaCl (Fig. 12c–d). The fluorescence intensity of bacteria in 0.9% NaCl after incubation with ST-JH was higher than that of ST-JHCQDs-180 at the same concentration, which was opposite to the fluorescence brightness in the confocal pictures. Since the sum of fluorescence intensities in saline originated from the exterior and interior of the bacteria with ST-JHCQDs-180 (or ST-JH), we speculate that the opposite fluorescence is due to the advantage of ST-JHCQDs as small nanomaterials and bioimaging, promoting more curcumin molecules to be taken up by the bacteria, and thus the interior of the bacteria incubated with ST-JHCQDs-180 exhibited stronger green fluorescence. These phenomena allow the resulting single-linear state to effectively diffuse into the cell membrane or penetrate directly into the bacterial cell, favoring the antibacterial performance of PDT [62].

The biocompatibility of ST-JHCQDs was evaluated by cytotoxicity and hemolysis tests. As shown in Fig. 13 a, the survival rate of cells treated with 5 μg/mL ST-JHCQDs was more than 75%. ST-JHCQDs-180 combined with blue light had a significant photoinactivation effect on S aureus at this solubility. Meanwhile, we also found that when the concentration was 25 and 50 μg/mL, compared with ST-JH, the survival rate of cells treated with ST-JHCQDs was improved, indicating that ST-JHCQDs are beneficial in reducing the cytotoxicity of turmeric alcohol extracts to some extent. In addition, hemolytic activity of ST-JHCQDs on erythrocytes was low, and no significant hemolysis occurred in erythrocytes treated with ST-JHCQDs.

From the terms of photodynamic antibacterial, ST-JHCQDs-180 prepared under low temperature conditions possessed more efficient photodynamic antibacterial effect compared with ST-JHCQDs-200 and ST-JHCQDs-220. As the irradiation time was extended to 40 min, only 5 μg/mL and 50 μg/mL of ST-JHCQDs-180 were required to inactivate 99.9% of S. aureus and E. coli, respectively. Meanwhile, we observed that ST-JHCQDs-180 was significantly more sensitive to S. aureus than E. coli. Secondly, in terms of biocompatibility, ST-JHCQDs-180, ST-JHCQDs-200 and ST-JHCQDs-220 gradually exhibited cytotoxicity as their concentrations were higher than 10 μg/mL. Among them, only ST-JHCQDs-180 at 5 μg/mL not only inactivated 99.9% of S. aureus by photodynamic action, but also showed a survival rate of more than 75% for cells. In contrast, ST-JHCQDs-180 requires higher concentrations when facing E. coli. Taking into account the safety and effective photodynamic antibacterial effect of ST-JHCQDs, ST-JH being prepared as ST-JHCQDs-180 and used to kill S. aureus under the specified concentration (5 μg/mL) and blue light irradiation (40 min) conditions was the best choice.

In this work, a series of ST-JHCQDs were prepared from turmeric, a natural plant, by extraction and solvent method at different temperatures, and demonstrated their photodynamic inactivation against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria in vitro. The antibacterial tests showed that the inactivation effect of ST-JHCQDs combined with blue light against bacteria depended on the variations of carbonation temperature, concentration of ST-JHCQDs and light exposure time, in which the photodynamic antibacterial performance of ST-JHCQDs diminished with the increase of carbonation temperature. Next, the photodynamic inactivation mechanism of ST-JHCQDs can be attributed to the exposure of ST-JHCQDs with blue light, which generates ROS capable of damaging bacterial cell membranes as well as leakage of cellular inclusions, leading to bacterial death. Meanwhile, ST-JHCQDs were better taken up by E. coli and S. aureus relative to turmeric extracts, thus enabling fluorescence imaging of bacteria. In addition, biocompatibility experiments showed that the hemolytic activity of ST-JHCQDs was low. In summary, the synthesis of ST-JHCQDs is simple, economical, and environmentally friendly, which has great potential for development as a new Nano-photosensitizer material for photodynamic applications.
